Part 1 Score 0 / 29

Question 1 3 pts

The collection of an account receivable (trade debtor) has the following impact:

(a) Increase income
(b) Increase cash flow from operations
(c) Decreases liabilities
(d) Increase total assets
(e) None of the above
Answer: (b)
When you collect an AR: Dr Cash, Cr Accounts Receivable. One asset swaps for another — total assets unchanged, no income effect, liabilities unaffected.

Cash flow from operations increases. Under the indirect method, the decrease in AR is a positive working-capital adjustment. Under the direct method, it is a cash receipt from customers.

Question 2 3 pts

Which of the following are generally treated as assets, rather than expenses?

(a) Cost incurred in improving the efficiency of a machine
(b) Advertising costs
(c) Cost incurred in supporting and developing the value of a key brand
(d) a and c
(e) None of the above
Answer: (a) IAS 16
(a) Capitalised — Improving a machine's efficiency enhances the asset beyond its original condition. Under IAS 16, this is capitalised.
(b) Expensed — Advertising costs are always expensed as incurred (IAS 38.69).
(c) Expensed — Internally generated brands cannot be recognised as intangible assets (IAS 38.63).

Only (a) is an asset. Option (d) is wrong because (c) is not an asset.

Question 3 3 pts

Suppose that a firm starts capitalizing R&D costs that should have been expensed. As a result:

(a) Assets and shareholders' equity will be higher
(b) Cash flows from operations will be higher
(c) Revenues will be higher
(d) a and b
(e) a, b and c
Answer: (d) — a and b IAS 38
(a) TRUE — Capitalising means higher assets and higher retained earnings (lower expense = higher NI = higher SE).
(b) TRUE — Cash outflow reclassified from operating to investing, so OCF is higher.
(c) FALSE — Capitalising R&D has no effect on revenue.

Question 4 3 pts

Why is depreciation added back to net income in an indirect cash flow statement?

(a) To increase cash flow artificially
(b) Because it is a non-cash expense that reduces net income
(c) To account for the cost of purchasing new equipment
(d) To reflect cash payments made for asset maintenance
(e) To equate to EBITDA
Answer: (b)
Depreciation reduced net income but involved no cash outflow. Adding it back reverses that non-cash deduction so OCF reflects actual cash generated.

Question 5 4 pts

On January 1, 2020, AlphaTech purchased a copyright for £50,000 (legal life 25 years, useful life 10 years). On December 31, 2023, the copyright was impaired to £0. What total expense did AlphaTech recognise in fiscal year 2023?

(a) £20,000
(b) £30,000
(c) £5,000
(d) £15,000
(e) £35,000
Answer: (e) £35,000 IAS 38 IAS 36
StepCalculationAmount
Annual amortisation50,000 / 10 yrs£5,000
Accum. (2020-2022)5,000 x 3£15,000
NBV at 1 Jan 202350,000 - 15,000£35,000
2023 amortisation£5,000
Impairment30,000 - 0£30,000
Total 20235,000 + 30,000£35,000

Question 6 3 pts

On 1 October 2023, Buffalo PLC paid £120,000 for product liability insurance for 24 months ending 30 September 2025. Recorded as an asset. Impact on the income statement for the fiscal year ending 31 December 2024?

(a) £60,000 insurance expense
(b) £45,000 insurance expense
(c) £15,000 insurance expense
(d) £120,000 insurance expense
(e) No effect
Answer: (a) £60,000
Monthly cost = £120,000 / 24 = £5,000/month.
FY 2024 = Jan-Dec = 12 months. Expense = 12 x £5,000 = £60,000.

Question 7 3 pts

Software development costs:

(a) Are never capitalized
(b) Are always capitalized
(c) Are expensed under US GAAP and capitalized under IFRS
(d) Under IFRS, expensed only if "technical feasibility" not established; US GAAP always capitalizes
(e) Under both IFRS and GAAP, capitalized once "technical feasibility" is established
Answer: (e) IFRS US GAAP
Under IAS 38: capitalised once six criteria are met (including technical feasibility).
Under ASC 350-40: capitalised once past the preliminary stage. Both require capitalisation after feasibility; costs before that are expensed.

Question 8 3 pts

On 31 August, Kelce Bros paid £9,000 for one year of rent in advance (lease starting 1 September). Which accounts and amounts appear on financial statements for the year ended 31 December?

(a) Prepaid Rent of £9,000
(b) Prepaid Rent of £3,000
(c) Rent Expense of £6,000
(d) Rent Expense of £3,000
(e) Both b and c
Answer: (d) Rent Expense of £3,000
Monthly = £9,000 / 12 = £750. Sep-Dec = 4 months. Expense = 4 x £750 = £3,000.
Prepaid at year-end = £6,000 (not listed as an option). (e) is wrong because both (b) and (c) have incorrect amounts.

Question 9 4 pts

On January 1, 2021, Wolverine plc purchased a patent for £360,000 (valid 10 years, useful life 5 years). On December 31, 2023, the patent was determined worthless. What amount of loss should Wolverine recognise for the year ended December 31, 2023?

(a) £216,000
(b) £288,000
(c) £36,000
(d) £72,000
(e) £0
Answer: (a) £216,000 IAS 38 IAS 36
StepCalculationAmount
Annual amortisation360,000 / 5 yrs£72,000
Accum. (2021-2022)72,000 x 2£144,000
NBV at 1 Jan 2023360,000 - 144,000£216,000
2023 amortisation£72,000
Impairment£144,000
Total 202372,000 + 144,000£216,000
Diageo plc is a British multinational alcoholic beverage company headquartered in London, operating from 132 sites worldwide. It is a major distributor of Scotch whisky and other spirits, and its brands include Johnnie Walker, Guinness, Smirnoff, Baileys, Captain Morgan, Tanqueray, and Don Julio. Use the excerpts from Diageo's 2022 annual report (year ended 30 June 2022) below to answer the following questions.

Diageo 2022 Annual Report — Financial Statement Excerpts

Assumptions: All amounts in £ millions. No impairment of long-term assets has been recorded over the years. All PP&E acquisitions paid in cash; all PP&E sales collected in cash. Straight-line depreciation with zero residual value.

Question 1 4 pts Cash Flow Statement

During fiscal year 2022, how much did Diageo record in depreciation, amortisation, and impairment expenses? What effect did these expenses have on Diageo's operating cash flows?

Look for the D&A add-back line in the operating activities section of the CF statement. For the OCF effect, think about what the indirect method does: it starts with profit, then adds back non-cash items. What's the net impact of an expense that reduces profit but is then added back?

Solution

From the CF statement, "Depreciation, amortisation and impairment" = £828 million.

Effect on OCF: Zero (net).
D&A reduces profit (-£828m) but is added back in full (+£828m) in the indirect method. These cancel out — no net effect on OCF.

Question 2 5 pts Income Statement + Balance Sheet

Estimate how much cash Diageo collected from their customers during fiscal year 2022. Ignore any cash collected related to excise duties.

Use the formula: Cash collected = Net sales - Increase in trade receivables. Find net sales (after excise duties) on the income statement, then find opening and closing trade receivables on the balance sheet. The increase in AR represents revenue earned but not yet collected in cash.

Solution

ItemSource£m
Net sales (excl. excise duties)Income statement15,452
Trade receivables 2022Balance sheet2,933
Trade receivables 2021Balance sheet2,385
Increase in AR2,933 - 2,385548
Cash collected15,452 - 548£14,904m

Question 3 5 pts Provisions Note

How much expense, if any, did Diageo recognise on their income statement related to their total provisions?

In the provisions note, identify which line items flow through the income statement vs. which are non-IS movements. "Charged during the year" is an operating expense. "Unwinding of discounts" is a finance cost (time value of money on long-term provisions). Utilisations are cash payments, not new expenses. Exchange differences and transfers are reclassifications.

Solution

Item£mIncome statement?
Provisions charged65Yes — operating expense
Unwinding of discounts5Yes — finance cost
Provisions utilised(89)No — cash payment
Exchange differences18No — OCI/equity
Transfers / disposals6No — reclassification
Total IS expense65 + 5 = £70 million

Question 4 5 pts PP&E Note + Cash Flow Statement

Assume that Diageo did not sell any computer software during the year ended 30 June 2022. Estimate the gain or loss Diageo recognised from selling Property, Plant, and Equipment during the year.

Gain/Loss = Proceeds - Net Book Value. Find the proceeds from the investing section of the CF statement ("Disposal of PP&E and computer software"). Find the NBV by looking at the PP&E note: Disposals row in the cost section minus Disposals row in the depreciation section (use the total column for both).

Solution

StepSource£m
Cost of PP&E disposedNote 10 — cost disposals (total)237
Accum. dep. reversedNote 10 — dep disposals (total)(218)
NBV of assets sold237 - 21819
ProceedsCF statement (investing)17
Gain / (Loss)17 - 19Loss of £2m

Question 5 5 pts Balance Sheet + Cash Flow Statement

Examining the balance sheet, how does the change in inventories during the year ended 30 June 2022 compare with the change in inventories listed on the cash flow statement? Provide possible reasons for any difference.

Compare two numbers: (1) the BS change = closing inventory minus opening inventory, and (2) the CF statement working capital adjustment for inventories. The CF statement only captures cash-related inventory changes. Think about what else could change inventory balances without involving operating cash (foreign exchange? acquisitions?).

Solution

MeasureSource£m
Inventories 2022Balance sheet7,094
Inventories 2021Balance sheet6,045
BS change7,094 - 6,0451,049
CF adjustment"Increase in inventories"(740)
Difference1,049 - 740£309m
Reasons:
1. FX translation effects — overseas inventory revalued in £ on BS, but FX is not a cash flow.
2. Business acquisitions/disposals — inventory from acquisitions goes on BS but is an investing (not operating) cash flow.
3. Hyperinflation adjustments (Turkey) — affect BS but not cash.

Question 6 6 pts PP&E Note 10 (footnote a)

At 30 June 2022, estimate the average remaining useful life for Diageo's Buildings and Equipment. State any assumptions you make and show your work.

Average remaining useful life = Depreciable NBV / Annual depreciation charge. For buildings, you must exclude non-depreciable land. Check footnote (a) at the bottom of Note 10 for the land amount. The depreciation charge for each category is in the depreciation movements section of the note.

Solution

Exclude non-depreciable land: Footnote (a) states "Depreciation was not charged on £114 million of land."

BuildingsEquipment
Carrying amount1,9032,847
Less: land(114)—
Depreciable NBV1,7892,847
Depreciation charge127277
Remaining life14.1 years10.3 years

Question 1 9 pts IFRS 15 IAS 20

In 2020, the UK Government developed an "Eat Out to Help Out" Scheme to support the reopening of restaurants and pubs after the COVID-19 lockdown. Under the scheme, the government funded a 50% discount on meals (up to £10 per diner) for sit-down meals on Mondays, Wednesdays and Tuesdays during August 2020. The government reimbursed restaurants for the discounted portion. Discuss the accounting issues for the restaurants participating in this scheme.

Think about two standards: IFRS 15 (revenue recognition — what's the transaction price?) and IAS 20 (government grants — how to present the reimbursement). The restaurant earns the full meal price from two sources: the customer's payment and the government's reimbursement. Under IAS 20, there are two presentation choices for grants related to income.

Solution

1. Revenue Recognition (IFRS 15):
Revenue = full meal price (customer + government portions combined). The restaurant is entitled to the full economic benefit.

2. Government Grant (IAS 20):
The reimbursement is a grant related to income. Two presentation choices:
• Gross: show grant as other income
• Net: deduct from related expense (e.g., cost of sales)

3. Timing: Recognise when conditions are met (meal served on eligible day). Record a receivable until government pays.

4. Disclosure: Nature, extent, and accounting policy for grants must be disclosed.

Question 2 10 pts IAS 37 IAS 10

On 13 December 2024, the press announced that Ofcom (Office for Communications) had fined UK's Royal Mail £10.5 million for delayed mail/parcel deliveries. Discuss the impact of these news events on Royal Mail's financial statements for the years ending 31 December 2023 and 31 December 2024.

Apply the IAS 37 framework: (1) Is there a present obligation from a past event? (2) Is outflow probable? (3) Can it be reliably estimated? If all three are met = provision. If only possible = contingent liability (disclose only). Also consider IAS 10: is the Dec 2024 announcement an adjusting event (provides evidence of conditions at year-end 2023) or non-adjusting?

Solution

Year ended 31 December 2023:
• If Ofcom investigation was already underway → the obligating event (service failures) occurred before 31 Dec 2023.
• If outcome was probable and estimable → recognise a provision (IAS 37).
• If outcome was only possible → disclose as contingent liability in notes.
• The Dec 2024 announcement may be an adjusting event (IAS 10) if it evidences conditions existing at 31 Dec 2023.

Year ended 31 December 2024:
• Recognise £10.5m as a liability and expense (or charge against existing provision).
• If no provision existed at 31 Dec 2023, the full £10.5m is a 2024 regulatory penalty expense.

Question 3 14 pts IAS 38

Consider the following footnote disclosure from a company's annual report. Explain how software costs are treated under International Financial Reporting Standards (International GAAP). Using the footnote, estimate the effect of expensing all software costs on the following accounts. For each, state whether the amount would be higher or lower and by how much. Assume all software costs were paid in cash.

Total assets Cash flows from investing Net income Retained earnings

Footnote: Property and Equipment, Net (Note 8)

If always expensed: Software's net book value would disappear from the BS. To estimate software NBV, you need software's share of accumulated D&A. Since software is ~94% of total gross PP&E, allocate accumulated D&A proportionally. For net income, compare what you'd expense (full $112m additions) vs what's currently charged (software's share of D&A + impairment). For retained earnings, the cumulative effect equals the current NBV.

Solution

IFRS Treatment: Under IAS 38, research costs are expensed; development costs (including software) are capitalised once six criteria are met (technical feasibility, intention to complete, ability to use/sell, future economic benefits, adequate resources, reliable measurement). Capitalised software is amortised over its useful life.

Key data from footnote:
• Software gross: $293,467k / Total gross: $313,277k = 93.7% software
• Software accum. D&A (est): 93.7% x $120,727k = ~$113,100k
• Software NBV: $293,467k - $113,100k = ~$180,400k
• 2023 capitalised: $112.0m | D&A: $70.0m | Impairment: $18.3m

AccountHigher/LowerAmountWhy
Total assetsLOWER~$180mSoftware NBV removed from BS
CFIHIGHER~$112mSoftware spend moves from investing to operating
Net incomeLOWER~$28mExpense $112m, save ~$66m D&A + ~$18m impairment
Retained earningsLOWER~$180mCumulative effect = software NBV
Note: CFO would be LOWER by ~$112m (cash outflow shifts from investing to operating). Total cash flow is unchanged.

Question 4 8 pts

There are many topics in the first five sessions of this course that you spent time studying that were not asked in this exam. What is one and how does it work? Choose a financial accounting topic and provide a thorough explanation demonstrating your understanding.

Choose a topic you know well and can explain thoroughly. Good options: bonds (effective interest method — discount vs premium, amortisation schedule), IFRS 16 leases (right-of-use asset, lease liability, interest expense vs depreciation), inventory (FIFO vs weighted average under IFRS — no LIFO), or revenue recognition (IFRS 15 five-step model). Show you understand the journal entries and BS/IS impact.

Example: Bond Accounting (Effective Interest Method)

IFRS 9

When a company issues bonds at a discount or premium to face value:
• Initially recorded at proceeds received.
• Each period: Interest expense = carrying amount x effective rate.
• Cash coupon = face value x coupon rate.
• Difference amortises the discount/premium.

Example: £100,000 face, 5% coupon, issued at £95,000 (effective rate ~6.2%), 5-year term.
• Year 1: Interest expense = 95,000 x 6.2% = £5,890. Cash paid = 100,000 x 5% = £5,000. Discount amortised = £890. New carrying amount = £95,890.
• At maturity, carrying amount converges to £100,000.
